title: Carolina Payeras - PSCI 3200 Final Project
section: This site hosts my final project for PSCI 3200.
text: This site hosts my final project for PSCI 3200.

title: Project Proposal Carolina Payeras
text:
I am very interested in development in Colombia, specifically the impacts of the guerilla presence in the rainforests and the rural areas. My proposal for a research question is: Is there an association between increases in poverty in rural areas of Colombia and increases in paramilitary/guerilla activity? I am curious to study this relationship to, via this project, investigate the relationship between rural poverty and violence. Another possible question that could come of this could be: Do increases in investment in rural areas of Colombia decrease guerilla activity?
For data sets, I was thinking of using the following:
Colombian conflict data from HDX https://data.humdata.org/dataset/colombia-acled-conflict-data
Specific variables: Admin1, Admin2, Events, Fatalities
Colombian poverty data from the World Bank: https://data.humdata.org/dataset/colombia-pobreza-multidimensional-departamental
Specific variables: Incidencia de Pobreza Multidimensional en IPM Departamentos para ver la pobreza por región de Colombia

title: Research Design Final Project
text:
Research Background:
For over 50 years, Colombia’s government has been engaged in a civil war with the guerrilla fighters known as the FARC (Revolutionary Armed Forces of Colombia), which has resulted in violence and fear around the country, as well as notorious drug-trafficking. In 2016, President Juan Manuel Santos signed a historic peace treaty with the FARC in the hopes of ending the conflict, with the FARC agreeing to disarm and transition into a legitimate political party and bringing about hope for a new Colombia. In 2018, however, successive President Iván Duque, who was a critic of the peace treaty, promised to ‘modify’ the treaty to be more punitive of former guerrillas, claiming the 2016 Treaty was too lenient. This change in position of the Colombian government only heightened tensions with the FARC, and the peace deal began to crumble as guerrilla forces resumed armed violence, and consequently the new branch of the FARC-EP gained momentum (Gutierrez D., 2020).
The current Colombian government under President Gustavo Petro announced their policy of ‘Total Peace’ in 2022, which proposed easing hostility towards the guerrillas in order to de-escalate violence and encourage negotiations and dialogue. The idea was that decreasing tensions and promoting communication would facilitate and environment that could bring both parties to an agreement. However, after four years of the Petro administration, the positive effects of Total Peace and the enormous undertaking it has been for the government have been under scrutiny by organizations such as the Washington Office on Latin America, who claim that the size and scope of the project has overwhelmed the government and undermined the policy’s success (2025).
The question this paper aims to investigate is whether Gustavo Petro’s Total Peace policy was successful in curtailing guerrilla-based violence in states with histories of FARC activity. This paper hypothesizes that in terms of total incidences of guerilla-based violence, these will have decreased after the implementation of Total Peace. I expect to see this relationship based on the policy being a large-scale government effort that aims to reduce hostility from the government towards the guerrillas, and in theory this will lead to fewer confrontations between armed parties, and potentially fewer violent incidences. If the FARC responded violently when they felt their voices in government where being silenced and demonized, then maybe this effort would ease their concerns of ostricization and political disenfrahnchisement. This is not to say that it will overall decrease guerrilla activity, which has actively been contested by scholars and institutions such as the ACLED (2024), but specifically it could likely reduce guerrilla violence.
The data used for this investigation was gathered by the ACLED and published on the United Nations Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs’ (OCHA) Humanitarian Data Exchange website. Specifically, the data focuses on incidences of violence against civilians per municipality, measured in events and fatalities from these violent incidences, on a weekly frequency. For the purposes of this investigation, we will only look at the number of incidences compositely on a year to year basis, and also by state, also known as departamentos, rather than by individual municipality for analysis purposes.
The variables from the data set that are used are Admin1 (departamentos), fatalities, events, and year. Yearly fatalities were calculated by summing weekly fatalities per year, and categorized by the departamento in which they took place; the unit of analysis therefore is departamento-year.


















These two plots look quite similar so I eventually may just choose to use the events one because it generally encompasses slightly more instances of violence, and I am looking to get the most accurate numbers possible.
The regression model for this analysis models the relationship between time post-Total Peace, or post-2022, and the number of violent incidents, which could be events or fatalities. Assuming a linear model, we would use the following formula:
\[
Violence_{dt} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 Post2022_t + \epsilon_{dt}
\]
The variables in the model are as follows:

Violence = Number of violent events or fatalities
t = time after Total Peace implementation in 2022
d = departamento
beta0 = intercept of the model
beta1 = coefficient of change in violence for every year post-2022
edt = this variable stands in for fixed-effects from each region that need to be controlled for, such as density, poverty, or region-specific shocks

title: Final Research Design
text: The question this paper aims to investigate is whether Gustavo Petro’s Total Peace policy led to a decrease in curtailing civilian-targeted violence in states with histories of FARC activity. This paper hypothesizes that  total guerilla-based violence will have decreased after the implementation of Total Peace. I expect to see this relationship based on the policy being a large-scale government effort that aims to reduce hostility from the government towards the guerrillas, and the mechanism through which the policy would reduce violence towards civilians would be fewer state-FARC confrontations and improved bargaining conditions. If the FARC responded violently when they felt their voices in government were being silenced and demonized, then maybe this effort would ease their concerns of ostracization and political disenfranchisement. This is not to say that it will overall decrease guerrilla activity, which has actively been contested by scholars and institutions such as the ACLED (2024), but specifically it could likely reduce violent events and fatalities

title: Final Research Design
text:
For over 50 years, Colombia’s government has been engaged in a civil war with the guerrilla fighters known as the FARC (Revolutionary Armed Forces of Colombia), which has resulted in violence and fear around the country, as well as notorious drug-trafficking. In 2016, President Juan Manuel Santos signed a historic peace treaty with the FARC in the hopes of ending the conflict, with the FARC agreeing to disarm and transition into a legitimate political party and bringing about hope for a new Colombia. In 2018, however, successive President Iván Duque, who was a critic of the peace treaty, promised to ‘modify’ the treaty to be more punitive of former guerrillas, claiming the 2016 Treaty was too lenient. This change in position of the Colombian government only heightened tensions with the FARC, and the peace deal began to crumble as guerrilla forces resumed armed violence, and consequently the new branch of the FARC-EP gained momentum (Gutiérrez D. 2020).
The current Colombian government under President Gustavo Petro announced their policy of “Total Peace” in 2022, which proposed easing hostility towards the guerrillas in order to de-escalate violence and encourage negotiations and dialogue. However, after four years of the Petro administration, the positive effects of Total Peace and the enormous undertaking it has been for the government have been under scrutiny by organizations such as the Washington Office on Latin America, who claim that the size and scope of the project has overwhelmed the government and undermined the policy’s success (“‘Total Peace’ Paradox in Colombia: Petro’s Policy Reduced Violence, but Armed Groups Grew Stronger | ACLED” 2025).
The challenges associated with re-integration of a society post-war are the subject of numerous academic works, and this investigation aims to build off of existing discourse. In their comprehensive article “Civil War”, Blattmann and Miguel explain that post-intrastate conflict, “victors and vanquished (and victims) are condemned to coexist in the same society, potentially exacerbating political and social divisions.” (Blattman and Miguel 2010) This could pose a challenge to Total Peace’s panacea of a solution in which ex-FARc fighters will be welcomed back into society, as not only could there still be emotional resistance towards cooperation with the state, social fear of FARC fighters and hostility towards them will not go away immediately with Petro’s policy.
Furthermore, an article touching on this exact point of interest was recently authored by Julián Arévalo, who positions Colombia’s current negotiations within the wider context of how peace negotiations after violence should be studied, emphasizing that elements within negotiations, rather than just the act of negotiating itself, are pivotal to its success (Arévalo 2025). Although this investigation will not dive into the details of the plan itself, the language and manner in which Petro is approaching peace could also be integral to its success in decreasing the intensity of violence. Finally, measuring Total Peace’s success first via intensity of conflict rather than conflict gives us a chance to engage with smaller levels of success within a complex peace process rather than a crude view of violence trends in their entirety. Building off of work by Bara et.al, who propose investigating various forms of post-war violence discretely to gain a clearer picture of a society re-integrating their previous adversaries (Bara, Deglow, and Baalen 2021).


The question this paper aims to investigate is whether Gustavo Petro’s Total Peace policy led to a decrease in curtailing civilian-targeted fatalities in states with histories of FARC activity. This paper hypothesizes that civilian-targeted fatalities will have decreased after the implementation of Total Peace. For this investigation, fatalities are used as a proxy for the intensity of violence rather than simply the occurrence of violence. I expect to see this relationship based on the policy being a large-scale government effort that aims to reduce hostility from the government towards the guerrillas, and the mechanism through which the policy would reduce violence towards civilians would be fewer state-FARC confrontations and less of a need to enforce dominance through killings. If the FARC responded violently when they felt their voices in government were being silenced and demonized, then maybe this effort would ease their concerns of ostracization and political disenfranchisement. This is not to say that it will overall civilian-targeted violence, which has actively been contested by scholars and institutions such as the ACLED (2024), but specifically it could likely reduce violent events and fatalities.

title: Final Research Design
text: The question this paper aims to investigate is whether Gustavo Petro’s Total Peace policy led to a decrease in curtailing civilian-targeted fatalities in states with histories of FARC activity. This paper hypothesizes that civilian-targeted fatalities will have decreased after the implementation of Total Peace. For this investigation, fatalities are used as a proxy for the intensity of violence rather than simply the occurrence of violence. I expect to see this relationship based on the policy being a large-scale government effort that aims to reduce hostility from the government towards the guerrillas, and the mechanism through which the policy would reduce violence towards civilians would be fewer state-FARC confrontations and less of a need to enforce dominance through killings. If the FARC responded violently when they felt their voices in government were being silenced and demonized, then maybe this effort would ease their concerns of ostracization and political disenfranchisement. This is not to say that it will overall civilian-targeted violence, which has actively been contested by scholars and institutions such as the ACLED (2024), but specifically it could likely reduce violent events and fatalities.

title: Final Research Design
text:
Main regression model
The regression model for this analysis models the relationship between time post-Total Peace, or post-2022, and the number of civilian-targeted fatilities. Assuming a linear model, we would use the following formula:
\[
Violence_{dt} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 Post2022_t + \alpha_d + \epsilon_{dt}
\]
The variables in the model are as follows:

Violence = Number of violent events or fatalities
t = time after Total Peace implementation in 2022 (in years)
d = departamento
Beta (0) = intercept of the model
beta1Beta (1) = coefficient of change in violence for every year post-2022
alpha (d) = this variable controls for stable differences across departamentos such as population density, poverty, and regional economic output
Edt = this variable stands in for time-dependent effects from each region that need to be controlled for, such as density, poverty, or region-specific shocks

Within this model, we want to focus on the value of Beta (1); should it be negative, this would confirm the hypothesis that the Total Peace Plan did decrease civilian-targeted fatalities in the chosen regions, even controlling for stable and time-dependent variation among the areas.

title: Final Research Design
text:
One empirical extension
One way that we can further validate that Total Peace is in fact working to decrease civilian-targeted fatalities is by seeing if coca production increased since Petro took office. In theory, less government oversight and patrol of FARC-controlled areas would mean higher coca production and fewer encounters with the state. The data used comes from the United Nations Office on Drugs and Crime.









NULL


As seen in Figure 2, coca production does indeed continue to increase when the Total Peace plan is put into place, which shows that the mechanism of reducing fatalities via backing off of FARC territory and activity could be a viable causal argument.
